mainText:
**Brexit Echoes in Starmer’s Resignation**
On Monday, less than two years after winning an electoral landslide that ended 14 years of Conservative rule, Sir Keir Starmer stood outside 10 Downing Street and announced his resignation.

“Every decision I’ve taken has been about putting the country I love first,” he said. “That is why I will resign as leader of the Labour Party.”

In his time in office, Starmer struck a trade agreement with India, Britain’s most significant deal since leaving the European Union; secured lower tariff rates from the Trump administration; cut waiting lists at the National Health Service (NHS) to their lowest in three and a half years; and pushed for the biggest planning overhaul in decades to facilitate homebuilding.

However, despite a parliamentary majority, Starmer was unable to make gains on other longstanding issues and stumbled on unpopular new policies, such as tax hikes on farms, a proposed digital work ID that drew a petition of nearly three million signatures opposing it, and a cut to a winter fuel subsidy he was forced to reverse. Voters made their displeasure known.

In May, the populist anti-immigration party Reform UK dominated local elections, gaining more than 1,250 council seats while Labour lost around 1,120 seats and control of 28 local councils. Recently, several Cabinet members resigned, making Starmer’s position untenable. Last week, when Andy Burnham, Labour’s popular mayor of Manchester and a prime ministerial hopeful, won a by-election to represent Makerfield and return to Parliament, the path was open for a leadership challenge—and so Starmer resigned.

Starmer is the sixth prime minister in the decade since the Brexit vote, a decision that its proponents promised would deliver economic growth and tighter borders. It has delivered neither and has wrecked both of Britain’s major political parties instead.

**Alan Greenspan Dies at 100**
Alan Greenspan, the former Federal Reserve chairman dubbed “maestro” for his understanding of financial markets, died Monday of complications related to Parkinson’s disease. He was 100 years old. Greenspan became chairman in 1987 and retired in 2006, making him the second-longest serving chair in Fed history. The “Black Monday” stock market crash happened roughly two months into his first term, and his quick shift to an expansionary monetary policy is credited with averting a crisis. He aggressively rooted out inflation with preemptive rate hikes, presiding over what was then the longest sustained period of economic growth in American history. Despite his Republican politics, as Fed chair he largely resisted partisanship but drew the ire of President George H.W. Bush for raising interest rates.

Greenspan was regarded as highly successful until the 2008 financial crisis led some to claim that his belief in letting markets correct themselves had helped bring on the crisis. He later admitted the crisis put him "in a state of shocked disbelief." His wife Andrea Mitchell commented on his passing, stating he was “a giant of a man who helped shape the U.S. economy for decades under presidents of both parties.”

**SCOTUS Reimposes Etan Patz Conviction**
The Supreme Court on Monday tossed out a lower-court decision that called for a retrial in the conviction of Pedro Hernandez for the infamous 1979 murder and kidnapping of Etan Patz, one of the first missing children featured on milk cartons. Hernandez was convicted in 2017 after approximately seven hours of police questioning, during which he was never advised of his Miranda rights.

**Judge Blocks White House Minnesota Subpoenas**
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration’s efforts to subpoena Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz and other state officials, finding that the Justice Department was using its authority to retaliate against local officials who did not cooperate with federal efforts to police illegal immigration. U.S. District Judge Patrick Schiltz ruled that the Constitution prevents the federal government from compelling states to enforce federal law.

**Spain Convicts Former Government Official on Bribery and Corruption Charges**
